elements.sent-2411-001: Summarised in this document is the Technical Design Report for the SABRE (Sodium Iodide with Active Background REjection) South experiment.
elements.sent-2411-002: SABRE South is a NaI(Tl) dark matter direct detection experiment, to be situated underground in the Stawell Underground Physics Laboratory, and aims to test the results from the DAMA/LIBRA experiment.
elements.sent-2411-003: SABRE South's three key sub-detectors are described, alongside other major components of the experiment design such as data acquisition, and shielding.
elements.sent-2411-004: The expected sensitivity of the detector to a DAMA/LIBRA-like signal is also discussed.
elements.sent-2411-005: A wide range of astrophysical evidence supports the existence of non-luminous matter known as dark matter (DM).
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on these observations, dark matter must have the following properties: it interacts gravitationally, but is electrically neutral and does not interact via the strong force; it is largely non-relativistic (``cold''); it is around five times more abundant than baryonic matter; it has a freezeout time that allows for the observed structural evolution of the universe; and, it is stable on cosmological timescales.
elements.sent-2411-007: Any observable signal from direct detection should have a distinct modulation signature due to the Earth's revolution around the Sun as it moves through the galactic dark matter halo.
elements.sent-2411-008: This modulation is unrelated to the Earth's seasons and is identical in both hemispheres.
elements.sent-2411-009: To be compatible with DM, the modulation signal should satisfy the following conditions: the modulation should have a period of one year; the modulation's phase should produce a peak at the start of June; for GeV-scale dark matter particle(s), the modulation should be present in a low energy range (keV-scale); the dark matter particle interacts rarely with matter, expecting only single hits with the target material; the modulation amplitude is not larger than $\sim$30\% of the total dark matter signal (the exact amount varies depending on the particle interaction model).
elements.sent-2411-010: The primary motivation of SABRE is to test a long-standing claim of a signal of dark matter interactions by another sodium iodide-based detector called DAMA / LIBRA, which has a significance of 13.7$\sigma$~\cite{Bernabei:2023gsv .}
elements.sent-2411-011: If the signal observed by DAMA/LIBRA originates from a seasonally modulating background, SABRE South has the ability to identify or exclude the DAMA/LIBRA claim, due to its location in the Southern Hemisphere.
elements.sent-2411-012: SABRE South is the Southern Hemisphere component of the full SABRE concept, which also consists of the SABRE North experiment in the Northern Hemisphere.
elements.sent-2411-013: By using ultra-pure crystals with a background lower than those used in DAMA/LIBRA, and twin detectors in both hemispheres, SABRE aims to test the DAMA/LIBRA dark matter signal.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Dark Matter searches in NaI(Tl) detectors
elements.sent-2411-014: DAMA/LIBRA, like its predecessor DAMA/NaI, aims to observe the model-independent modulation of a dark matter interaction rate.
elements.sent-2411-015: The most recent iteration, DAMA/LIBRA-phase 2 (and phase 1), used 250 kg of NaI(Tl) over 8 years of data collection.
elements.sent-2411-016: Combining this with previous experiments, DAMA/LIBRA has a total exposure period of 2.86 tonne years.
elements.sent-2411-017: The interaction rate itself, integrated over a given energy window $k$, can be given by \begin{equation}R_k(t) \approx R_{0,k} + S_{m,k} \cos(\omega(t - t_0)),\end{equation} where $R_{0,k}$ is the constant rate, $S_{m,k}$ is the amplitude of the modulating component of the rate, $\omega$ is the angular frequency, and $t_0$ is the phase \cite{BERNABEI2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The DAMA collaboration has consistently observed a modulating signal over their background rate of 0.8~cpd/kg/keV~\cite{BERNABEI2020 , compatible with a dark matter signal over the DAMA/NaI, DAMA/LIBRA phase 1 and DAMA/LIBRA phase 2 experiments in the 2-6~keV region, this has a modulation amplitude of $0.01014 \pm 0.00074$~cpd/kg/keV with a phase and period of 142.4~$\pm$~4.2 days and 0.99834~$\pm$~0.00067~years respectively~\cite{bernabei2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the 1-6~keV recoil energy range (reported for DAMA/LIBRA-phase 2 only) this modulation occurs with an amplitude of $0.01058 \pm 0.00090$ cpd/kg/keV, period of $0.99882\pm0.00065$~years and phase of $144.5 \pm 5.1$~days.
elements.sent-2411-020: Recent preliminary results indicate that this modulation persists below the 1~keV threshold~\cite{Bernabei:2023gsv .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The signal observed by DAMA/LIBRA is in direct tension with a wide variety of direct-detection dark matter experiments using different target materials~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, there have also been studies suggesting that DAMA/LIBRA's background averaging technique (if performed over a time-dependent background) could have induced a modulating result~\cite{COSINEInducedMod .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, after reproducing a best estimate of their background, we find that: (i) the reproduced background is too low for a modulation to be induced on the same scale as their reported signal, and (ii) the resulting induced modulation is opposite in phase to the modulation reported by DAMA/LIBRA~\cite{SABREInducedMod .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, in recent years, other NaI(Tl)-based experiments have been developed to test this modulation using the same target as DAMA, such as COSINE~\cite{Adhikari_2021 and ANAIS~\cite{Coarasa:2024xec .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We list the observed modulation from each of the operating experiments in Table~\ref{tab:global_mod .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Results in the 1-6~keV region for COSINE and ANAIS are known to be impacted by higher than expected background contributions that are unexplained \cite{Adhikari2021, Coarasa:2024xec ; a potential cause of this is photomultiplier noise~\cite{ANAISBDT .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Additionally, the signal efficiencies of the event selection criteria drop steeply as 1~keV is approached.
elements.sent-2411-028: For example, between 1-2~keV, the ANAIS detection efficiency drops to as low as 20\%, with the degree of background rejection not reported~\cite{Coarasa:2024xec .}
elements.sent-2411-029: As a result, a comparison of results in the 2-6~keV region is likely more robust.
elements.sent-2411-030: To definitively test DAMALIBRA, SABRE must be sensitive to a modulation of 0.01 cpd / kg / keV, which requires a total background of no more than 1 cpd/kg/keV, and ideally better than the DAMA value of 0.8 cpd/kg/keV~\cite{BERNABEI2020 .}

We define dru (differential rate units) as cpd/kg/keV. } \label{tab:global_mod} \begin{tabular}{@{}lllll@{}} Setup Mass (kg) Bkg. (dru) $S_m$ (1-6~keV) (dru) $S_m$ (2-6~keV) (dru) DAMA/LIBRA phase 2 250 0.8 ~0.0105 $\pm$ 0.00090 0.0093 $\pm$ 0.00094 COSINE 61.3 2.7 ~0.0067 $\pm$ 0.0042 0.0051 $\pm$ 0.0047 ANAIS 112.5 3.2 ~0.0007 $\pm$ 0.0025 0.0030 $\pm$ 0.0025 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-031: The SABRE South detector is hosted at the Stawell Underground Physics Laboratory (SUPL), which is 1025 m underground in Victoria, Australia.
elements.sent-2411-032: SUPL is the first deep underground laboratory in the southern hemisphere.
elements.sent-2411-033: The laboratory has a flat overburden of basalt, providing approximately 2900~m of water-equivalent shielding, and a muon flux of $\sim 3 \times 10^{-8}$~cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$(Fig.~\ref{fig:supl ).}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{supl.png} \caption{Left: location of SUPL. Right: muon flux at a number of underground laboratories as a function of water equivalent depth. The point for SUPL is based on simulated results using GEANT4 and MUTE~\cite{MUTE}.} \label{fig:supl}
elements.sent-2411-034: The construction of SUPL was completed in 2023, with the first access for SABRE South installation activities in early 2024.
elements.sent-2411-035: Assembly and commissioning of the main detector components in SUPL is currently scheduled for 2025, after which data collection will begin.
elements.sent-2411-036: SUPL has helical drive access through mining tunnels, which has implications for how large detector components are transported and designed.
elements.sent-2411-037: The walls of SUPL, covered with a pre-screened low background concrete mixture, were finished with two coats of Tekflex sealant to inhibit radon flow into the lab through the walls.
elements.sent-2411-038: A radon-reduction system was part of the original SUPL design; however, due to financial constraints, the system has not yet been implemented.
elements.sent-2411-039: The airflow into the SUPL air-conditioning system arrives through approximately 9~km of decline tunnels, drawn down by large fans pumping air at high rate through separate vent shafts.
elements.sent-2411-040: This air and other inlet air pick up radon gas from the untreated walls along the way.
elements.sent-2411-041: A compressed air line carrying air from the surface to the depth of SUPL will be used to feed an over-pressure of low-radon air into clean-tent environments within SUPL during assembly of the detector.
elements.sent-2411-042: The measured radon activity in the laboratory is $415 \pm 5$~Bq/m$^{3}$, which requires special precautions during the assembly and design of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-043: This includes the need to avoid radon decay daughter products, such as $^{210}$Pb, from depositing (``plating'') onto sensitive parts of the detectors and related equipment.
elements.sent-2411-044: A schematic of the laboratory (including the location of SABRE South) is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:supl-schem , alongside a photograph of the laboratory.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The main hall has floor dimensions of 10~m by 26~m and a maximum height of 12~m.
elements.sent-2411-046: On the side of the main hall are the cleaning facilities and entry way.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{SUPLLabelled.png} \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{SUPL_for_tdr.jpg} \caption{Left: Schematic of SUPL including the main experimental hall (top left), low background counting (HPGe) facilities (top middle), loading bay (top right), and the entry way with cleaning facilities (bottom left). Right: Photograph of SUPL. SABRE South will be located near the centre of the image. The door on the left leads to the rest areas and entry way.} \label{fig:supl-schem}
elements.sent-2411-047: To definitively test the DAMA/LIBRA anomaly, not only does the same target material need to be used, but an ultra low background is required in order to be sensitive to the 0.01~cpd/kg/keV reported modulation amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-048: With the main component of the total background being the radiogenic background in the crystal, we aim to bring the background of the SABRE South experiment to levels comparable with those of DAMA/LIBRA by ensuring high NaI(Tl) radiopurity.
elements.sent-2411-049: Furthermore, by employing a background veto system, in the form of both the liquid scintillator detector and muon detector, external background and remaining radioimpurities can be tagged and removed.
elements.sent-2411-050: The test crystals grown for the SABRE collaboration are competitive with similar NaI(Tl) in terms of their radiopurity \cite{Amare2019, Adhikari, Suerfu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: As indicated in Refs.~\cite{Adhikari, Amare2019 , and also shown by SABRE South simulations in Ref.~\cite{sabre_background , radiogenic backgrounds intrinsic to the crystals dominate the total background, meaning radiopure crystals are the key factor in achieving a low-background experiment.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The purity levels of the DAMA, ANAIS, COSINE, and SABRE crystals are listed in Table~\ref{tab:competition , with the predicted SABRE South energy spectrum (with the veto efficiency applied) shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sabre_crys_bkg .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Some of the background contributions are suppressed by the active veto system, while others need to be reduced through purification processes applied during the growth of the crystals.
elements.sent-2411-054: The SABRE crystals already offer a large reduction in $^{40}$K and, with the exception of DAMA, have the lowest levels of $^{210}$Pb.
elements.sent-2411-055: Further crystal production consistent with these results would see SABRE becoming the lowest-background NaI(Tl) dark matter detector worldwide, and is, by design, the driving factor behind the 0.72~cpd/kg/keV total background reported in Ref.~\cite{sabre_background .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Further details on NaI (Tl) background modelling for SABRE South can be found in Ref.~\cite{sabre_background .}
elements.sent-2411-057: SABRE South is considering two different final scenarios regarding crystal growth: one with a total mass of $\sim$~50 kg (7$\times$7 kg crystals grown using the Bridgman method by the Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS)), and another with a total mass of 35 kg (7$\times$5 kg crystals grown using Zone Refining followed by the Bridgman method by Radiation Monitoring Devices (RMD), Boston).
elements.sent-2411-058: References to a total active mass of 35-50 kg are due to the consideration of these different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-059: While the total background and sensitivity of the experiment for a 50~kg scenario is discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:sensitivity , the 35~kg scenario has tighter background requirements to maintain the same sensitivity.}
elements.sent-2411-060: For 35~kg, an external plus cosmogenic background of 0.23~cpd/kg/keV and an intrinsic background of 0.53~cpd/keg/keV is required for 3$\sigma$ exclusion, while a 0.27~cpd/keg/keV intrinsic background (with the same external+cosmogenic background) is required for 5$\sigma$ discovery.
elements.sent-2411-061: We have assessed that the expected intrinsic background for the 35~kg scenario will be $<$0.3~cpd/kg/keV.

elements.sent-2411-062: The SABRE South experiment is designed to detect dark matter with a 35-50 kg NaI(Tl) target and an active background rejection system.
elements.sent-2411-063: The apparatus is made up of three sub-detector systems: (i) the NaI(Tl) crystal detector array, (ii) the linear alkylbenzene (LAB) liquid scintillator veto system, and (iii) the plastic scintillator muon veto system.
elements.sent-2411-064: Together, the liquid scintillator and muon detectors act as an active veto, rejecting events from intrinsic and extrinsic background sources.
elements.sent-2411-065: These components are shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:sabre-schem and are detailed in the following sections.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{sabre_south_annotated.jpg} \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{copper_enclosure_bits.jpg} \caption{Top: Schematic view of the full SABRE South detector. Bottom: Schematic view of the crystal detector system where OFHC copper refers to oxygen free high thermal conductivity copper.} \label{fig:sabre-schem}
elements.sent-2411-066: The NaI(Tl) crystal detector system is the core of the detector, and significant effort has been put into photomultiplier tube (PMT) characterisation, crystal growth, enclosure design, and insertion system design.
elements.sent-2411-067: The crystals are grown from Merck (formerly Sigma-Aldrich) Astrograde NaI powder, which has potassium contamination below 10~ppb \cite{suerfu-thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Upon arrival at SUPL, they are stored underground and assembled in a nitrogen-flushed assembly glove box, housed in a radon- and dust-reduced space.
elements.sent-2411-069: SABRE collaborates with SICCAS (China) and RMD (USA) to produce ultra-pure NaI(Tl) crystals.
elements.sent-2411-070: RMD has produced several prototypes that were tested at LNGS (Laboratori Nazionali del Gran Sasso) for optical and radiopurity properties.
elements.sent-2411-071: SICCAS is currently growing additional test crystals.
elements.sent-2411-072: Low levels of natural radiation within the NaI powder and other detector materials constitute a key background component.
elements.sent-2411-073: These can produce energy depositions in the region of interest (1-6~keV) that obscure the DM signal.
elements.sent-2411-074: The isotopes that form significant background near the region of interest are $^{40}$K and $^{210}$Pb, alongside others depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:sabre_crys_bkg .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Note that the $^{87}$Rb contribution is from an upper limit measurement and is expected to be significantly lower in practice.
elements.sent-2411-076: Each crystal is coupled to two 76 mm-diameter Hamamatsu R11065 PMTs, and is enclosed in an oxygen-free, high-thermal-conductivity (OFHC) copper enclosure.
elements.sent-2411-077: These crystal enclosures are then submerged in the SABRE South vessel, suspended by copper conduits that house the cables from the PMTs inside the vessel.
elements.sent-2411-078: They have been fabricated at the Australian National University and have been designed to be leak-proof when immersed in LAB for the duration of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-079: To ensure a moisture-, oxygen-, and radon-free volume, the enclosures will also be flushed with high-purity nitrogen, the handling and control of which will be managed by a bespoke gas handling system (see Sec.~\ref{sec:GHS ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: After assembly, each crystal detector will undergo preliminary testing in a low-radon, nitrogen purged, shielded environment in SUPL, to ensure good performance and make initial measurements of the intrinsic (radiogenic and cosmogenic) background.
elements.sent-2411-081: After insertion into the veto vessel, in-situ characterisation and full background measurements will be performed.
elements.sent-2411-082: A summary of the components is given in Table~\ref{tab:sabre-specs-nai .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Components of SABRE South NaI(Tl) detector system including their specifications.} \label{tab:sabre-specs-nai} \begin{tabularx}{0.99\textwidth}{l l} \bf{Component} \bf{Specifications} NaI(Tl) crystals 7 $\times$ 5-7 kg - length $\sim$15-20~cm, dia.~$\sim$10~cm Photomultipliers Hamamatsu R11065, 76 mm dia., 2 per crystal, QE $>$ 30\% Readout CAEN V1730 digitiser, 500~Ms/s sample rate, 0.12~mV resolution Enclosure OFHC copper shell and internal structure with PTFE (Teflon) components, sealed with viton O-ring \end{tabularx}
elements.sent-2411-083: Following earlier collaboration with Hamamatsu on the development of an ultra-low radioactive background in a PMT, SABRE South has procured the full set of 14 R11065 76~mm PMTs with $\ge30\%$ quantum efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-084: The single-photon response, dark rate, and afterpulse rate of each PMT are being characterised at The University of Melbourne prior to installation.
elements.sent-2411-085: SABRE South supports seven crystal detector modules.
elements.sent-2411-086: The enclosures are designed to ensure the aforementioned contaminant-free environment during handling, assembly, and insertion of the NaI(Tl) detector modules.
elements.sent-2411-087: A render of a crystal enclosure, with the associated conduit/support pipe, is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:CEFullRend .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Each enclosure is suspended by an enclosure support pipe that is connected to the top endcap in the top flange of the veto vessel.
elements.sent-2411-089: It carries the PMT signal and high voltage (HV) connections, gas lines, and other sensor cables, while keeping them isolated from the liquid scintillator (LS).
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{CE_full_render.png} \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{CE_exploded.png} \caption{A render of a crystal enclosure, with the crystal and PMTs depicted. The enclosure will attach to a copper conduit/support pipe, also pictured. An exploded render of the crystal enclosure is also shown.} \label{fig:CEFullRend}
elements.sent-2411-090: The enclosures must meet a strict set of requirements.
elements.sent-2411-091: The leak rate across the sealing surfaces can not exceed $1\times10^{-6}$~mbar$\cdot$L /s.
elements.sent-2411-092: They are to be immersed in the LAB LS for at least 5 years without degradation of the LS or ingress of the scintillator into the enclosure.
elements.sent-2411-093: Long-term material compatibility studies performed by SABRE South have confirmed that all materials in direct contact with the LS are compatible and are not expected to break down.
elements.sent-2411-094: The enclosures must provide a minimal contribution to the experimental background.
elements.sent-2411-095: The material between the crystals and the LS must be minimised for optimal veto efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-096: The dimensions of the enclosures must comply with the restrictions imposed by the crystal insertion system and the veto vessel top flange.
elements.sent-2411-097: The crystal dimensions are expected to vary slightly in size, so the enclosures must be adaptable enough to accommodate a range of sizes.
elements.sent-2411-098: The design allows a height of up to 250~mm, and a diameter limit of approximately 110~mm, corresponding to an 8.72~kg crystal.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, the assembly of the modules is performed inside a glovebox, so the design must accommodate assembly with a restricted range of motion.
elements.sent-2411-100: The enclosures are made entirely of OFHC copper (C0100) and PTFE.
elements.sent-2411-101: The OFHC copper components are extremely radiopure while being commercially available.
elements.sent-2411-102: The outer shell of the crystal enclosure has an outer diameter of 143~mm, a maximum wall thickness of 3~mm and a length of 670~mm.
elements.sent-2411-103: The hermetic seals of the enclosure are achieved using `V-grooves' that compress O-rings with a 45$^\circ$ chamber in the endcap plates against the copper shell.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{enclosure_composite.png} \caption{A render of the full internal subassembly supporting the NaI(Tl) crystal and coupled PMTs, with components labelled.} \label{fig:CrysSubAssemb}
elements.sent-2411-104: Shown and fully labelled in Fig.~\ref{fig:CrysSubAssemb , the internal crystal sub-assembly is designed to mechanically support the NaI(Tl) crystal and the coupled PMTs within the copper shell, ensuring that coupling is maintained during experimental run time and through the later stages of assembly.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The crystal is held with two PTFE holders that sit above and below the crystal with a cutout for the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-106: These are connected with three OFHC-copper compression rods that provide mechanical support for the crystal-PMT coupling.
elements.sent-2411-107: Each PMT is held in place with a copper and PTFE holder, which is connected to the compression rods.
elements.sent-2411-108: This design provides an evenly distributed pressure on the PMTs and the optical grease, which ensures good optical coupling to the NaI(Tl) crystals without causing excessive stress on the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-109: The compression rods are 7~mm in diameter with partial threading to attach the PMT holders, to allow for variations in crystal height.
elements.sent-2411-110: The crystal sub-assembly is connected to the enclosure endcaps with three copper columns that, when assembled, provide the mechanical support for the sub-assembly, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:CEFullRend .}
elements.sent-2411-111: As the crystal growth process may lead to crystals of differing dimensions, the crystal enclosure has been designed to accommodate variations both in crystal height and diameter.
elements.sent-2411-112: Consequently, there are components that are either independent of or dependent on the size of the crystal.
elements.sent-2411-113: The main components dependent on size are the compression rods and copper support columns.
elements.sent-2411-114: These are made to the crystal dimensions and are designed to align the crystal and veto vessel midplanes.
elements.sent-2411-115: Crystals with a height less than the limit will have more clearance at each end.
elements.sent-2411-116: The remaining components are independent of crystal size and allow for modular manufacturing and assembly prior to the crystal growth being finalised.
elements.sent-2411-117: In addition, a top endcap was manufactured to conduct leak testing using a helium leak checker, which will be repeated for each manufactured enclosure.
elements.sent-2411-118: The crystal enclosure components independent of crystal size have since been manufactured.
elements.sent-2411-119: The crystal enclosures are assembled in a glove box from Xiamen LITH Machine Limited.
elements.sent-2411-120: It has a main chamber 1800~mm in length with four workstations, and an 800~mm ante-chamber for loading the crystals and components for assembly.
elements.sent-2411-121: The glove box is constructed from 3~mm stainless steel, and has 8~mm-thick plexiglass windows.
elements.sent-2411-122: Organic solvents can be used within the glove box for polishing of the crystals, should they be required.
elements.sent-2411-123: The ante-chamber can be pumped under vacuum and backfilled with N$_2$, and the main chamber operates with a constant flow of N$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-124: The expected concentration of O$_2$ and H$_2$O is at the ppm level.
elements.sent-2411-125: Only PMTs with quantum efficiency (QE) $>30\%$ were procured, so to achieve a high single-photon detection efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-126: Custom PMT voltage dividers are used to minimise their contribution to the background.
elements.sent-2411-127: These bases will follow the resistance ratios recommended by Hamamatsu for this type of PMT.
elements.sent-2411-128: SABRE South has decided on a design that will use a positively biased voltage divider.
elements.sent-2411-129: The circuit diagram and an image of the PCB for the voltage divider design are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:CrysPMTBase .}
elements.sent-2411-130: The final design is expected to use small ceramic capacitors.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.425\textwidth]{CrysBaseDesign.png} \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{CrysPCBBase.jpg} \caption{Left: The circuit diagram for the final R110065 base design. Right: One of the PCBs to be used for the base circuit, which will be attached to an R11065 PMT.} \label{fig:CrysPMTBase}
elements.sent-2411-131: The pre-calibration program for the R11065 (crystal detector) PMTs focuses on confirming their single-photon detection efficiency, characterising their single-photo electron (PE) PMT response, and characterising the noise as a function of temperature and voltage.
elements.sent-2411-132: These parameters are used as an early calibration input to SABRE South data analysis and as an input into the digitisation stage of detector simulations.
elements.sent-2411-133: The requirements for a quantum efficiency larger than $30 \%$ (at a wavelength of 420~nm) and excellent signal-to-background discrimination for single photons are driven by the light yield requirements (of $\sim$~12-14~PE/keV) on the crystal detectors to achieve a threshold of 1-2 keV.
elements.sent-2411-134: Several essential measurements will be completed for all PMTs under strict cleanliness protocols.
elements.sent-2411-135: These are: relative quantum efficiency, gain, dark rate, and afterpulse rate at nominal voltage.
elements.sent-2411-136: Further characterisation of single-photon response and noise, including temperature and bias voltage dependence, is performed on a limited set of PMTs to limit overall exposure.
elements.sent-2411-137: As SABRE operates at room temperature, dark counts due to thermionic emission and temperature dependence of this dark rate are essential factors that are more easily measured in a dedicated setup than in-situ.
elements.sent-2411-138: The precalibration datasets allow for the development of data analysis techniques for discrimination of thermionic emission from low-energy events in the crystals, near the 1-2 keV region.
elements.sent-2411-139: There have previously been reports \cite{Barrow_2017,Akimov:2015alu,Akimov_2016 of light emission likely due to anode and dynode glow following a sufficiently intense light signal in the R11065 and the related R11410 PMT types.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Such light emission could introduce an additional background in SABRE and therefore needs to be characterised if present.
elements.sent-2411-141: All components of the crystal enclosures are cleaned and vacuum baked prior to final assembly to ensure that there is no moisture remaining on the components.

elements.sent-2411-189: SABRE South's active veto system serves to identify background events originating from radioactive decays within the crystals that may mimic a signal and to act as passive shielding for background external to the crystals.
elements.sent-2411-190: For example, $^{40}$K, a key impurity, decays via the emission of an Auger electron with an energy in the range of 1-6 keV coincdent with a 1.5~MeV $\gamma$ ray.
elements.sent-2411-191: Previous experiments have accounted for this by employing double-coincidence rejection, where it was assumed that the escaping gamma ray would be detected by another crystal in the sensitive mass~\cite{Bernabei:2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: However, this detection mechanism is not fully efficient, as demonstrated by the peak at around 3~keV in the DAMA data where events from $^{40}$K are expected~\cite{Bernabei2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: The key components of the LS veto detector are summarised in Table~\ref{tab:sabre-specs-veto .}
elements.sent-2411-194: By immersing the NaI(Tl) crystal detector modules in a liquid scintillator (Fig.~\ref{fig:active_veto ), high-energy $\gamma$ rays produced by radioactive decays can be detected and the decay event vetoed.}
elements.sent-2411-195: The SABRE South full background simulation demonstrated that the veto can reduce background from important $^{40}$K contamination by a factor of 10 in the region of interest, and suppresses the total experimental background by 27\%~\cite{sabre_background .}

elements.sent-2411-269: The PMTs are gain matched in each detector module and monitored using radioactive source-based calibration and muon signals.
elements.sent-2411-270: The SABRE South sub-detectors require periodic calibration, which is particularly important for accurate time-dependent measurements.
elements.sent-2411-271: Calibration refers to the process of bias-correcting and verifying the accuracy of a detector's measurements.
elements.sent-2411-272: The LS veto and NaI(Tl) calibration systems for SABRE South are designed for remote operation, and are located within the shielding structure.
elements.sent-2411-273: The response of the detector to particle interactions can change with time, due to: (i) environmental changes such as temperature, humidity, mechanical stress and vibrations (recorded by the slow control system outlined in Sec.~\ref{sec:slowcontrol ); (ii) electrical changes: variations in the supply voltage, EM interference; (iii) detector ageing: over time optical properties of scintillators can deteriorate (e.g. reduced absorption length or light yield), and the PMTs can vary in detection efficiency and other features used for pulse shape discrimination.}
elements.sent-2411-274: SABRE South uses radioactive sources for the calibration of the NaI(Tl) detectors, the liquid scintillator veto, and the muon detector.
elements.sent-2411-275: The liquid scintillator veto is also calibrated with an optical (pulsed laser light) system.
elements.sent-2411-276: The two liquid scintillator veto calibration systems are complementary, with the radioactive calibration system providing accurate high-energy calibration lines and the optical system capable of sending low-intensity high-frequency pulses to test the single photoelectron and timing response of the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-277: Calibration with radioactive sources requires data runs of a few hours every time a calibration run is scheduled (other NaI(Tl) experiments have 3--4 hour calibration runs every 2 weeks~\cite{Amare2019eff ).}
elements.sent-2411-278: The laser calibration can be run more frequently in short bursts as it does not require any mechanical adjustments to the system.
elements.sent-2411-279: The frequency of calibration of the two systems will be adjusted as necessary to optimise for detector stability and live-time.
elements.sent-2411-280: Radioactive sources must be placed near the detectors in order to calibrate their responses.
elements.sent-2411-281: During these periods (calibration runs), the signal induced by the calibration source must be strong enough to be distinguishable from the other detected signals.
elements.sent-2411-282: Conversely, during normal data acquisition, the signal induced by the calibration source must be negligible.
elements.sent-2411-283: This is achieved by keeping the sources shielded and at a distance from the detectors during normal operations and by remotely moving the sources to positions nearby the detectors only during calibration runs.
elements.sent-2411-284: SABRE South uses motorised calibration deployment systems for the calibration of both the crystal and the liquid scintillator detectors, denoted RCS1 and RCS2, respectively (Fig.~\ref{fig:RCS ).}
elements.sent-2411-285: Each system is composed of a steel mount, a stepper motor, a calibration probe suspended through a wire, an alignment reel, and sensors for position measurement.
elements.sent-2411-286: The stepper motor is used to wind and unwind a wire around a spool, thus moving the calibration probe up and down.
elements.sent-2411-287: The wire passes around a centring reel, so that the probe can accurately slide into an aluminium pipe, which guides it to the calibration position.
elements.sent-2411-288: The systems are controlled by the online slow control system using the EtherCAT protocol and are capable of moving the radioactive sources between the calibration position and the normal operation position in minutes.
elements.sent-2411-289: No human intervention is needed in the experiment during operation.
elements.sent-2411-290: Access will only be required in the unexpected case of parts failure.

elements.sent-2411-302: The veto detector system is monitored and calibrated by using optical light from a pulsed laser system: specifically the PMT timing, gain and resolution, optical properties of the LAB scintillator, and overall detector stability.
elements.sent-2411-303: The conceptual design of the optical calibration system uses optical fibres sheathed in Teflon tubes that carry picosecond laser pulses to designated emission positions within the veto vessel.
elements.sent-2411-305: Figure~\ref{fig:OpticalCalibration2 shows two of the considered configurations, where light is emitted from one and three diffusive light sources, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-306: Quartz fibres are chosen to transmit the light signal from the laser into the vessel as the material is chemically compatible with LAB.
elements.sent-2411-307: The optical fibres are sheathed with Teflon, to increase the reflectivity and improve signal isotropy, and fed into the veto vessel via one or more ConFlat 151.6~mm outer diameter nozzles (depending on the configuration).
elements.sent-2411-308: The two optical calibration configurations were simulated with Geant4, to analyse the proportion of photoelectrons collected by the PMTs, and to examine the photon flight times, trajectories in the LAB, and their probability of survival.
elements.sent-2411-309: These configurations differ in the number of optical fibres and where they terminate.
elements.sent-2411-310: In both configurations, the fibres run along the edge of the vessel and terminate into a diffusive bulb held in place by a tripod.
elements.sent-2411-311: The diffuser bulbs are used to achieve an isotropic light source.
elements.sent-2411-312: For configuration 1, the bulb is located halfway between the bottom of the central enclosure and the bottom of the vessel, i.e. 65 cm directly below the centre of the vessel (which is the origin of the detector coordinate system).
elements.sent-2411-313: This position is optimised for the propagation of photons within the scintillator, as well as the number of photoelectrons detected by the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-314: For configuration 2, the bulbs are arranged into an equilateral triangle centred on the origin.
elements.sent-2411-315: The distance from the origin to each bulb is 69~cm.
elements.sent-2411-316: The diffuser bulbs are solid spheres of PTFE with a diameter of about 1.5~cm, which is proven solution for LS experiments~\cite{JunoOptCalib .}
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{OpticalCalibration1_crop.png} ~ \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{OpticalCalibration2_crop.png} \caption{Left: configuration 1, calibration with a single diffusive light source located halfway between the bottom of the central enclosure and the vessel floor. Right: configuration 2, three evenly-spaced diffusive light sources for better coverage and redundancy within the veto vessel. The path of the optical fibre from the vessel ConFlat nozzle to the light emitter is shown in blue. \label{fig:OpticalCalibration2}}
elements.sent-2411-317: The nominal light source is an LDB-200 picosecond pulsed laser from Tama Electric, and can be remotely controlled.
elements.sent-2411-318: A wavelength 405~nm was initially considered due to the high quantum efficiency of PMTs at this wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-319: However, the photon absorption length at this wavelength is 0.3~m.
elements.sent-2411-320: Therefore a laser wavelength of 445~nm, where the absorption length is 25~m, is chosen.
elements.sent-2411-321: The PMT quantum efficiency is only 5\% lower than at 405~nm.
elements.sent-2411-322: In the simulation we consider diffuse isotropic emission of mono-energetic photons from the diffusive bulb positions.
elements.sent-2411-323: For configuration 1, the proportion of photons captured in the veto PMTs is about 14$\%$.
elements.sent-2411-324: The PMTs located at the bottom of the vessel collect approximately double the number of photoelectrons than those at the top.
elements.sent-2411-325: Since this difference is not large, comparable calibration accuracy can be achieved for each unit.
elements.sent-2411-326: For configuration 2, the proportion of detected photons is the same as the single source option, but there is a smaller difference in photons collected by each PMT across the detector.
elements.sent-2411-327: This configuration offers more timing calibration data by having multiple source positions.
elements.sent-2411-328: The nominal 445~nm laser source can output up to $2\times10^{5}$ photons per pulse and reach a maximum pulse frequency of 100 MHz, allowing ample room for adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-329: Both configurations are valid options for the optical calibration system.
elements.sent-2411-330: As configuration 1 is the easiest option to implement, we consider it the primary candidate.
elements.sent-2411-331: A schematic layout of the Data Acquisition (DAQ) system is given in Fig.~\ref{fig:DAQ-SABRESystems as well as other related components that are discussed in more detail in later sections.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Signals from every PMT are digitised by CAEN digitisers, and the resulting data are then transferred to a computer and written to disk.
elements.sent-2411-333: Each of the SABRE subsystems (the crystal, liquid scintillator, and muon detectors) is managed largely independently with three DAQ instances collecting data in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-334: For the crystal subsystem, a dual-readout approach is being considered in which two signals from each PMT are digitised (amplified and unamplified).
elements.sent-2411-335: A CAEN FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array)-based board, running custom firmware, is used to coordinate triggering and provide synchronisation signals between the digitisers where required.
elements.sent-2411-336: A custom C++ program is responsible for managing the data collection from the digitisers.
elements.sent-2411-337: The system described here is operational in SUPL and used in precalibration (Fig.~\ref{fig:DAQ-DAQRack ).}
elements.sent-2411-338: Live data-taking systems are managed through custom run-control software built on React Automation Studio (RAS).
elements.sent-2411-339: This uses the networked publisher-subscriber interface provided by EPICS (Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System) to control and coordinate the various systems.
elements.sent-2411-340: The slow control interfaces through a dedicated extensible interface to EPICS for control and a database system for data archiving.

elements.sent-2411-342: Several CAEN digitisers are used to record the signals generated by the detector PMTs: the 14 crystal PMTs are digitised by two V1730 digitisers (8 channels per digitiser); the liquid scintillator PMTs use a V2730 digitiser (32 channels); and the 16 muon PMTs use a V1743 digitiser (16 channels per digitiser).
elements.sent-2411-343: Table~\ref{tab:digitisers summarises the digitisers used for each sub-detector.}
elements.figure-2411-028: \caption{Summary of digitisers used by each sub-detector.} \label{tab:digitisers} \begin{tabularx}{0.99\textwidth}{lXXXX} \bf{Digitiser} \bf{Channels} \bf{Sampling rate (S/s)} \bf{Resolution (bits)} \bf{LS veto} V2730 32 $500 \times 10^6$ 14 \bf{Crystal detectors} V1730 8 $500 \times 10^6$ 14 \bf{Muon veto} V1743 16 $3.2 \times 10^9$ 12 \end{tabularx}
elements.sent-2411-344: The CAEN V1730~\cite{CAEN_1730 is an 8-channel, 500~Ms/s digitiser with a 14-bit ADC and a 0.5/2~Vpp dynamic range.}
elements.sent-2411-345: It is capable of continuous acquisition with zero dead time.
elements.sent-2411-346: Whenever the digitiser is triggered, the time of the event is recorded along with waveforms for each channel and some meta-data.
elements.sent-2411-347: To achieve an energy threshold of 1 keV, the trigger must be sensitive to single photoelectron signals.
elements.sent-2411-348: The noise rate at such a threshold demands the use of coincidence triggers across pairs of PMTs coupled to the same crystal and configured as channel pairs on the digitiser.
elements.sent-2411-349: Used to record signals generated in the LS veto, the 32-channel V7230 digitiser samples at 500~MS/s with a 14-bit ADC and a dynamic range of 4~Vpp~\cite{CAEN_2730 .}
elements.sent-2411-350: The V2730 also has improved triggering options through custom FPGA firmware that this new generation of CAEN digitisers supports, allowing individual channel triggers, and correlated triggers between channels.
elements.sent-2411-351: The CAEN V1743~\cite{CAEN_1743 is a 16-channel, 3.2~GS/s digitiser with a 12-bit ADC and a 2.5~Vpp dynamic range.}
elements.sent-2411-352: It is used with the muon detector sub-system where the high-digitisation rate (and subsequent timing resolution) is useful for position reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-353: However, due to its high digitisation rate, the board has a significant amount of dead time (125~$\mu$s for 1024 samples) per event.
elements.sent-2411-354: Since the expected event rate is $\sim$10 Hz and previous work with the muon detectors has acquired the full 1024 samples, this dead time is not expected to have a significant impact.
elements.sent-2411-355: To coordinate triggering between the independent digitisers, a CAEN V2495~\cite{CAEN_2495 programmable logic unit (PLU) with a customisable FPGA is used.}
elements.sent-2411-356: The trigger conditions of interest to SABRE South include a multiplicity trigger (generated when multiple channels trigger in coincidence) and a singles pre-scale calibration trigger (where only a defined fraction of the single channel events triggered on will be saved).
elements.sent-2411-357: Additionally, the trigger system is used to provide a common start signal to each of the digitisers, synchronising the start of the acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-358: Custom trigger firmware has been developed.
elements.sent-2411-359: The high voltage to the PMTs is supplied using a CAEN SY5527 \cite{CAEN_5527 Universal Multichannel Power Supply System with A7435 \cite{CAEN_7435 power supplies which can supply up to 3.5 kV and 3.5 mA per channel.}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Two 24-channel, positive polarity power supplies will be used for the crystal detectors and LS veto, whilst a single 24-channel negative polarity power supply will be used for the muon veto.
elements.sent-2411-361: These power supplies have a typical voltage ripple of < 7~mVpp, and voltage set resolution of 5~mV~\cite{CAEN_7435 .}
elements.sent-2411-362: The mainframe has a built-in CPU controller to control and monitor each channel.
elements.sent-2411-363: The SY5527 mainframe has a built-in EPICS input-output controller (IOC).
elements.sent-2411-364: A custom program has been written to control the SABRE DAQ instances.
elements.sent-2411-365: Each DAQ system is controlled by independent instances of this program running on the corresponding DAQ computer.
elements.sent-2411-366: Control and monitoring of these instances is performed through EPICS.
elements.sent-2411-367: The control flow of the program is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-368: When a start signal is received, the program reads the configuration parameters and initialises each of the boards in the DAQ.
elements.sent-2411-369: It then enters a loop where each board is checked and any data present are saved directly to file (alternate output streams are also supported).
elements.sent-2411-370: This continues until either a stop signal is received from EPICS, a pre-set end condition is met (e.g. acquisition duration), or an error occurs.
elements.sent-2411-371: In addition to the raw data from the digitisers, the acquisition is monitored through a number of EPICS process variables, including event rates, data rates, and sample waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-372: Environmental sensors are used not only to monitor the health and performance of the experiment but also to ensure the health of experimenters and detector operators.
elements.sent-2411-373: An early prototype read-out system was developed using an NI-cRIO framework for this system \cite{Krishnan2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-374: Due to costs associated with expanding the system, an alternative EPICS-integrated and Ethercat-based system has been developed.
elements.sent-2411-375: The new alternative system uses Beckhoff couplers to digitise the analogue signals from each sensor; these are then read using the open-source EPICS Ethercat support module \cite{EPICS_Ethercat .}
elements.sent-2411-376: The sensors that SABRE South will use are listed below.
elements.sent-2411-377: Thermometers: Some of the instrumentation is sensitive to temperature, particularly the noise rate of the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-378: Thus, the temperatures of and within are monitored for later analysis.
elements.sent-2411-379: Resistance thermometer detector (RTD) sensors with four-wire PT100 (platinum 100 ohm) probes are used to measure the ambient laboratory temperature and the temperature of various parts of the SABRE South hardware.
elements.sent-2411-380: These sensors must be far enough from the crystals not to introduce excess background.
elements.sent-2411-381: Barometers: Air pressure within SUPL is monitored with a Delta Ohm-HD9408.3B piezoresistive sensor, with a resolution of 0.1 hPa.
elements.sent-2411-382: Pressure sensors are important for nitrogen-flushed systems (such as the crystal glove box) to ensure that an adequate flow and overpressure of nitrogen is maintained.
elements.sent-2411-383: Hygrometers: Relative humidity in the laboratory is monitored using an APAR-AR250 transducer, with a $\pm2$~\% accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-384: A relative humidity sensor is incorporated into the high-purity nitrogen lines that are used to flush the interior of the crystal enclosures and the vessel, to ensure that water is not introduced into the system.
elements.sent-2411-385: Liquid level sensors: Liquid level sensors are used in two locations: (i) near the top of the inside of the veto vessel to monitor the level of the liquid scintillator; and (ii) in a liquid catch tray beneath the vessel, to directly monitor for any possible leak of LAB from the vessel.
elements.sent-2411-386: Volatile organic compound (VOC) sensors: A VOC sensor is located within the shielding volume, such that any liquid LAB or LAB vapour leaking from the vessel will be immediately detected.
elements.sent-2411-387: An additional VOC sensor is part of the fluid-handling system to ensure that there are no LAB leaks in the crystal enclosures.
elements.sent-2411-388: Vibration accelerometers: As SUPL is located in an active gold mine, there are occasions when vibrations from blasts or heavy vehicles elsewhere in the mine may be transmitted through to SUPL, inducing noise in the PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-389: Data on such vibrations are obtained via VSA001 single-axis accelerometers.
elements.sent-2411-390: Three of these are be mounted independently on the outside of the veto vessel, with each sensor oriented to provide x/y/z measurements.
elements.sent-2411-391: Analogue current output (0-10 mA) is proportional to motion (sensitive from 1~Hz to 6~kHz vibration frequency).
elements.sent-2411-392: Radon: The level of radon in the SUPL environment will be monitored.
elements.sent-2411-393: During experiment operation, we are primarily concerned with radon in the vessel-shielding volume.
elements.sent-2411-394: This will be done via an AlphaGuard detector.
elements.sent-2411-395: Oxygen: Oxygen sensors are placed within the shielding (external to the veto vessel) and within the nitrogen blanket inside the veto vessel (above the LAB level).
elements.sent-2411-396: There may also be oxygen monitoring within the fluid handling system and general SUPL environment.
elements.sent-2411-397: This is necessary as oxygen can dissolve into the LS and consequently reduce the light yield.
elements.sent-2411-398: The key elements of the environmental background radiation that must be suppressed in the SUPL laboratory space are radioactive radon gas, neutrons (expected flux of $3-4 \times 10^{-5}$~cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$) and gamma rays (expected flux of 2.5~cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$) from radioactive decays in the rock and secondary products from cosmic-ray muon interactions in the rock.
elements.sent-2411-399: The liquid scintillator protects the crystals from external radiation, however additional passive shielding is required to: (i) bring the induced background to a negligible level in the crystals; (ii) keep the event rate in the veto low enough to ensure low dead-time; and to allow the use of the veto system for background characterisation and other physics studies such as detection of supernova neutrinos (for a supernova early warning system).
elements.sent-2411-400: The SABRE South detector has 112 tonnes of high purity steel and high-density polyethylene (HDPE) shielding.
elements.sent-2411-401: This is made of a 10~cm-thick layer of HDPE, to shield against neutrons, interposed between two 8~cm layers of steel to shield against high-energy gamma rays.
elements.sent-2411-402: This structure is a cube of approximately 4~m side-lengths, and surrounds the main SABRE South liquid scintillator vessel.
elements.sent-2411-403: This configuration was chosen over alternatives, such as stacked water containers or lead, as steel offers excellent structural strength, serves as an effective radiation shield, and is more compact.
elements.sent-2411-404: High purity steel made with over 80\% new steel can also be easily sourced in Australia.
elements.sent-2411-405: Through simulations, steel shielding has been found to attenuate as much radiation as lead shielding with half the thickness.
elements.sent-2411-406: Interposing steel and high-density polyethylene (HDPE) provides a relatively compact and structurally sound shield, with the HDPE effective in neutron thermalisation and capture.
elements.sent-2411-407: Figure~\ref{fig:Shielding provides a sketch of the shielding.}
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{Shielding1.png} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{Shielding3NoLabels.png} \caption{Left: Sketch of the radiation shielding with the internal support structure outline in red, and standing plate and roof section outline in white. Right: Cutaway of the shielding design, where the internal red layer represents that HDPE sandwiched by inner/outer layers of steel. The internal support frame is also depicted.} \label{fig:Shielding}
elements.sent-2411-408: Other configurations of the shielding layers were considered, using alternating layers of gamma-ray attenuator (lead) and neutron attenuator (HDPE) keeping a constant total thickness of the two attenuators.
elements.sent-2411-409: The more gamma-ray attenuator there is in the innermost part of the shielding, the better the shielding is at attenuating gamma-rays and the worse it is at attenuating neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-410: For each configuration, the effect on the contribution of external radiation to the crystal background is just a few percent.
elements.sent-2411-411: The main difference between configurations is the rate of low-energy radiation, which is effectively absorbed by the liquid scintillator.
elements.sent-2411-412: The optimal solution is therefore a configuration that is structurally preferable.
elements.sent-2411-413: The final configuration is expected to bring the expected background rate from external radiation down to $\mathcal{O}$($10^{-4}$)~cpd/kg/keV$_{\rm ee}$.
elements.sent-2411-414: Table~\ref{tab:shielding_activity outlines the radiopurity of the shielding components.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Samples of similar low-carbon aluminium-killed steel were screened by the Australian Nuclear Science and Technology Organisation (ANSTO) with a HPGe detector.
elements.sent-2411-416: No radioactive isotope was detected in the sample.
elements.sent-2411-417: This measurement was approximately ten times less sensitive than that used to detemine the measurements in Table~\ref{tab:bulk_steel , and therefore we consider conservative limits.}
elements.sent-2411-418: The radioactivity of the HDPE layers is not expected to contribute significantly to background, so HDPE samples were not measured.

elements.sent-2411-420: Outlined below are the projected performance and sensitivity of the SABRE South detector, based on Geant4 simulation and a complete background model in the region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-421: The key performance parameters are driven by the fact that, for NaI(Tl) crystals, a 1 keV deposition produces 30-45 photons, translating to 10-15 photoelectrons detected across the two PMTs in each module.
elements.sent-2411-422: The SABRE South region of interest therefore has a series of distinct single-photon pulses distributed over several hundreds of nanoseconds.
elements.sent-2411-423: All currently operating NaI(Tl) experiments have background models that poorly reproduce the data at energies below 3 keV \cite{ANAIS2021,BERNABEI2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-424: Part of the difficulty in modelling the background in this low-energy region is due to the challenges of separating PMT noise, caused by spontaneous thermionic emission of electrons, from a genuine scintillation event of interest.
elements.sent-2411-425: Table~\ref{tab:sabre-param , lists the performance parameters relevant to the projected sensitivity.}
elements.sent-2411-426: The quoted crystal light yield in this table is from measurements using PMTs with a QE lower than those in the final SABRE South detector.
elements.sent-2411-427: The light yield in SABRE South is expected to be approximately 20\% higher.

elements.sent-2411-428: Assuming a total mass of 50 kg and a background of 0.72 cpd/kg/keV (Fig.~\ref{fig:sensitivity ), the exclusion power and the discovery potential of SABRE South for the DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation signal \cite{Bernabei2018 are computed.}}
elements.sent-2411-429: The discovery potential of SABRE South to observe a signal with a modulation of 0.0119 cpd/kg/keV is calculated following the methodology of Ref.~\cite{Zurowski_2021 (Fig.~\ref{fig:sensitivity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-430: This methodology involves producing distributions of $S_m$ (the modulation amplitude or rate of modulating events) for background only and signal plus background scenarios, where these distributions are generated by sampling from a background-only Poisson distribution for each time bin (assuming no modulation) or a signal plus background Poisson distribution (assuming a modulating signal).
elements.sent-2411-431: The discovery or exclusion power is determined based on the difference between the means of these distributions scaled by either $1/\sigma_{b}$ or $1/\sigma_{sb}$ respectively (where each $\sigma_{b,sb}$ derives from the $S_m$ distributions).
elements.sent-2411-432: With 1.2 (3.1) annual cycles of data, SABRE South is able to refute the interpretation of the DAMA/LIBRA modulation as a dark matter signal with 3$\sigma$ (5$\sigma$) CL.
elements.sent-2411-433: For observation of annual modulation, the modulation signal would reach a significance of 5$\sigma$ CL with two full years of data collection.
elements.sent-2411-434: This is possible because of the lower background rate compared to that of DAMA/LIBRA, and it is driven mainly by the development and optimisation of ultra-pure crystals and the active veto.

Right: The exclusion and discovery power of SABRE South for a DAMA-like signal. The shaded regions indicate 1$\sigma$ statistical uncertainty bands.} \label{fig:sensitivity}
elements.sent-2411-435: This document presents the executive summary of the technical design of the SABRE South detector, a NaI(Tl)-based detector that aims to test the long-standing DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation result.
elements.sent-2411-436: Using the same target material as the DAMA/LIBRA detector, the SABRE South NaI(Tl) crystals have an ultra-low background, owing to growth from Merck Astrograde NaI powder (which has potassium contamination below 10 ppb).
elements.sent-2411-437: Crystal growth is ongoing through collaborations with SICCAS (China) and RMD (USA).
elements.sent-2411-438: Each crystal is coupled to two PMTs and enclosed in an oxygen-free, high-thermal-conductivity copper enclosure that is submerged in the SABRE South vessel (suspended by copper conduits).
elements.sent-2411-439: The vessel itself contains 11,600 L of liquid scintillator -- a mixture of LAB doped with approximately 3.5 g/L of PPO and 15 mg/L of bis-MSB.
elements.sent-2411-440: The vessel is instrumented with a baseline of 18 PMTs, and as many as 32.
elements.sent-2411-441: By including a LS veto detector, and submerging the crystal detector modules within in it, radioactive decays from within the crystals that may emit high-energy gamma rays can be tagged --- for example, $^{40}$K decays, which form a key impurity in the NaI(Tl) crystals.
elements.sent-2411-442: The LS veto detector can also veto external background, with the possibility to employ reconstruction methods to disentangle background sources.
elements.sent-2411-443: In addition to the LS veto, a 9.6 m$^2$ muon veto will be placed above the detector and shielding to veto incoming cosmogenic muons.
elements.sent-2411-444: This muon veto is also capable of position reconstruction of tagged muons along the length of the panels, and can be used in coincidence with the LS veto.
elements.sent-2411-445: The expected background rate for SABRE South in the 1-6~keV region is 0.72 cpd/kg/keV$_{\rm ee}$, which results in a 5$\sigma$ discovery (3$\sigma$ exclusion) power to a DAMA-like signal after 2 years of data-taking.
elements.sent-2411-446: The SABRE South experiment is being installed in the Stawell Underground Physics Laboratory (SUPL) in 2024/2025, with the muon detectors having already been commissioned in telescope-mode as of early 2024.

elements.sent-2411-452: We thank the SABRE North collaboration for their contributions to the SABRE South experiment design, engineering, NaI(Tl) crystal development, background screening, and simulation framework.
elements.sent-2411-453: SABRE South is a NaI(Tl) dark matter direct detection experiment, to be situated underground in the Stawell Underground Physics Laboratory in the Southern Hemisphere, and aims to test the results from the DAMA/LIBRA experiment.
elements.sent-2411-454: A wide range of astrophysical evidence supports the existence of non-luminous matter known as dark matter (DM)~\cite{Freese:2008cz, Froborg:2020tdh, Rubin:1970, Zwicky:1937zza, Planck:2015fie .}
elements.sent-2411-455: Based on these observations, dark matter must have the following properties: it interacts gravitationally, but is electrically neutral and does not interact via the strong force; it is largely non-relativistic (``cold''); it is around five times more abundant than baryonic matter; it has a freeze-out time that allows for the observed structural evolution of the universe; and, it is stable on cosmological timescales.
elements.sent-2411-456: Any observable signal from direct detection should have a distinct modulation signature due to the Earth's revolution around the Sun as it moves through the galactic dark matter halo~\cite{Froborg:2020tdh .}
elements.sent-2411-457: To be compatible with DM, the modulation signal should satisfy the following conditions~\cite{FreeseAnnualMod, Froborg:2020tdh : the modulation should have a period of one year; the modulation's phase should produce a peak at the start of June; for GeV-scale dark matter particle(s), the modulation should be present in a low energy range (keV-scale); the dark matter particle interacts rarely with matter, expecting only single hits with the target material; the modulation amplitude is not larger than $\sim$30\% of the total dark matter signal (the exact amount varies depending on the particle interaction model).}
elements.sent-2411-458: The primary motivation of SABRE (defined in Ref.~\cite{SABRE_POP , which details the prototype detector) is to test a long-standing claim of a signal of dark matter interactions by another sodium iodide-based detector called DAMA/LIBRA, which has a significance of 13.7$\sigma$~\cite{Bernabei:2023gsv .}}
elements.sent-2411-459: SABRE South is the Southern Hemisphere component of the full SABRE concept, which also consists of the SABRE North experiment~\cite{SABRENorth in the Northern Hemisphere.}
elements.sent-2411-460: SABRE South is capable of 5$\sigma$ CL exclusion after 3.1 years of data (see Sec.~\ref{sec:sensitivity ).}
elements.sent-2411-461: SABRE South's sensitivity to WIMPs is calculated in Refs.~\cite{Zurowski_2021, sabre_background , and has been compared to other NaI(Tl) experiments including DAMA/LIBRA.}
elements.sent-2411-462: The SABRE South detector is also capable of testing other models of dark matter and their interactions, as well as detecting supernova neutrinos out to the galactic centre, as secondary goals.
elements.sent-2411-463: Preliminary sensitivity studies have been performed for the detection of spin-dependent WIMPs via the Migdal effect~\cite{Migdal , the detection of bosonic super WIMPs~\cite{BSWs , and for the use of the SABRE South detector in a supernovae early warning system (SNEWS)~\cite{SNEWS:2020tbu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-464: Utilising the SABRE South liquid scintillator veto, we would expect to observe approximately 6 events from a typical core-collapse supernova event at 10~kpc, assuming a 3-fold coincidence requirement and the nominal number of PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-465: While the number of detection events would be smaller than other detectors in the SNEWS system, an additional experiment in the Southern Hemisphere, would be seen as valuable.
elements.sent-2411-466: Publications discussing the SABRE South detector's sensitivity to various dark matter models, and to supernovae neutrinos, are expected in the future.
elements.sent-2411-467: The most recent iteration, DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 used 250 kg of NaI(Tl) over 8 years of data collection.
elements.sent-2411-468: Figure~\ref{fig:wimpwind depicts how the modulation is induced by the Earth's motion through the WIMP wind.}

elements.sent-2411-469: The DAMA collaboration has consistently observed a modulating signal over their background rate of 0.8~cpd/kg/keV~\cite{BERNABEI2020 (counts per day/kg/keV) throughout the DAMA/NaI, DAMA/LIBRA-phase1 and DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 experiments in the 2--6~keV region, which has a modulation amplitude of $0.01014 \pm 0.00074$~cpd/kg/keV with a phase and period of 142.4~$\pm$~4.2 days and 0.99834~$\pm$~0.00067~years respectively~\cite{bernabei2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-470: In the 1--6~keV recoil energy range (reported for DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 only) this modulation occurs with an amplitude of $0.01058 \pm 0.00090$ cpd/kg/keV, period of $0.99882\pm0.00065$~years and phase of $144.5 \pm 5.1$~days.
elements.sent-2411-471: Therefore, in recent years, other NaI(Tl)-based experiments have been developed to test this modulation using the same target as DAMA, such as COSINE~\cite{COSINE2024 and ANAIS~\cite{Coarasa:2024xec .}}
elements.sent-2411-472: Results in the 1--6~keV region ANAIS are known to be impacted by higher than expected background contributions that are unexplained \cite{Adhikari2021, Coarasa:2024xec ; a potential cause of this is photomultiplier noise~\cite{ANAISBDT .}}
elements.sent-2411-473: For example, between 1--2~keV, the ANAIS detection efficiency drops to as low as 20\%, with the degree of background rejection not reported~\cite{Coarasa:2024xec .}
elements.sent-2411-474: For COSINE, the detection efficiency is around 40\% at 1~keV, with a sharp efficiency turn-on effect over the energy interval of 1--2~keV~\cite{COSINE-100LowThres , which may lead to non-negligible systematic uncertainties.}
elements.sent-2411-475: As a result, a comparison of results in the 2--6~keV region is likely more robust.

elements.sent-2411-478: The assembly and commissioning of the main detector components in SUPL is currently scheduled for 2025, after which data collection will begin.

elements.sent-2411-481: To definitively test the DAMA/LIBRA anomaly, it is essential to use the same target material and achieve an ultra-low background to detect the reported modulation amplitude of 0.01 cpd/kg/keV.
elements.sent-2411-482: Since the primary contributor to the total background is the radiogenic background in the crystals, the SABRE South experiment aims to achieve background levels comparable to those of DAMA/LIBRA by ensuring high NaI(Tl) radiopurity.
elements.sent-2411-483: Additionally, by employing a background veto system—comprising the liquid scintillator detector and the muon detector—external background and residual radioimpurities can be tagged and removed~\cite{Calaprice:2021yml .}
elements.sent-2411-484: The test crystals grown for the SABRE collaboration are very competitive with other NaI(Tl) crystals in terms of radiopurity~\cite{Amare2019, Adhikari, Suerfu2019, antonello2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-485: SABRE South's location in the Southern Hemisphere also offers a unique advantage by providing data that can disentangle seasonal effects.
elements.sent-2411-486: SABRE South and SABRE North are designed such that radiogenic background intrinsic to the crystals dominate the total background, as demonstrated through simulations~\cite{sabre_background, SABRE_MC and indicated in Refs.~\cite{Adhikari, Amare2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-487: This design highlights that radiopure crystals are the key factor in achieving an experiment capable of reproducing the DAMA/LIBRA sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-488: The purity levels of the DAMA, ANAIS, COSINE, and SABRE crystals are listed in Table~\ref{tab:competition , with the SABRE South crystal radiogenic background spectrum (Fig.~\ref{fig:sabre_crys_bkg ) taken from the measurements in Ref.~\cite{antonello2021 , which utilise the SABRE Proof-of-Principal crystal.}}}
elements.sent-2411-490: The SABRE crystals have a $^{40}$K contamination better than that of DAMA/LIBRA and much lower than than the other existing NaI(Tl) experiments (COSINE and ANAIS).
elements.sent-2411-491: Another important contaminant is $^{210}$Pb that can accidentally enter the crystal during powder production and handling, or in the growth procedure, due to exposure to $^{222}$Rn~\cite{antonello2021, Adhikari, Yu:2020ntl .}

elements.sent-2411-494: As shown in Ref.~\cite{antonello2021 , SABRE crystals have the lowest NaI(Tl) background worldwide, enabling SABRE to achieve the 0.72~cpd/kg/keV total background reported in Ref.~\cite{sabre_background .}}
elements.sent-2411-495: Further details on NaI(Tl) background modelling for SABRE South can be found in Ref.~\cite{sabre_background , and crystal characterisation is discussed in Ref.~\cite{antonello2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-496: SABRE South is considering two different final scenarios for crystal growth: one with a total mass of approximately 50 kg (7$\times$7 kg crystals grown using the Bridgman method by the Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS)), and another with a total mass of 35 kg (7$\times$5 kg crystals grown using zone refining followed by the Bridgman method by Radiation Monitoring Devices (RMD), Boston).
elements.sent-2411-497: References to a total active mass of 35-50 kg reflect these different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-498: In both cases the Bridgman method is chosen, as opposed to the Kyropoulos technique, as the molten raw material can be completely sealed inside the crucible during crystal growth, reducing the risk of contamination.
elements.sent-2411-499: The total background and sensitivity for the 50 kg scenario are discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:sensitivity , where the total background of 0.72~cpd/kg/keV (with the radiogenic crystal background of 0.52~cpd/kg/keV~\cite{sabre_background after the veto is applied) allows for a 5$\sigma$ exclusion in three years and a 5$\sigma$ discovery in two years.}}
elements.sent-2411-500: The 35 kg scenario requires stricter background limits to maintain the same sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-501: For the 35 kg case, to achieve a 5$\sigma$ discovery or 3$\sigma$ exclusion, a total background of less than 0.5~cpd/kg/keV is needed.
elements.sent-2411-502: Assuming the same NaI(Tl) cosmogenic (cosmogenic refers to activation from cosmic rays) and extrinsic contributions to the total background, this means the NaI(Tl) radiogenic background must be less than 0.3~cpd/kg/keV~\cite{sabre_background , which is achievable using the zone refining technique~\cite{SABRENorth .}}
